era: 1993-present. DJ/hip-hop turntablist. E=5 -- kinetic turntablist energy, physical performance, scratching and beat juggling. 206 Zulu. Central District.

location.formative: Central District -- historical heart of Seattle's Black community. Jackson Street jazz heritage. Hip-hop birthplace. Community centers, parks, block parties. 206 Zulu events and programming.
location.career: DJ Riz, 206 Zulu member (1993-present). Turntablist. Community DJ. Hip-hop cultural architect. Central District-rooted.

members.name: DJ Riz
members.note: Active since 1993. Central District. Turntablist -- scratching, beat juggling, mixing. 206 Zulu (Universal Zulu Nation PNW chapter). Three decades of continuous practice. Community DJ, not primarily radio. Closes the radio era (126-136).

associated_musicians.name: Nasty Nes Rodriguez
associated_musicians.note: KCMU hip-hop pioneer. E=4. The first hip-hop DJ in the engine. DJ Riz (136, E=5) continues the hip-hop lineage ten degrees later. Radio pioneer to community architect.

associated_musicians.name: DJ B-Mello
associated_musicians.note: KEXP Street Sounds. E=5. Three consecutive E=5 DJs: B-Mello (134), Indica Jones (135), DJ Riz (136). Radio to community transition.

associated_musicians.name: Indica Jones
associated_musicians.note: KEXP electronic. E=5. Capitol Hill. Same energy, different genre, different neighborhood. Electronic (Capitol Hill) vs. hip-hop (Central District).

associated_musicians.name: Sabzi
associated_musicians.note: Blue Scholars/Common Market producer. E=4. Different facet of Seattle hip-hop. Producer vs. turntablist. Both rooted in community.

associated_musicians.name: Cheryl Waters
associated_musicians.note: KEXP Midday. E=3. Opened the KEXP sextet (130). DJ Riz (136) closes the radio era. Broadcast to community. E=3 to E=5 arc.

theory_nodes.concept: Central District enters S36 as new neighborhood -- historical heart of Seattle's Black community; Jackson Street jazz heritage; hip-hop birthplace; gentrification and cultural persistence; the CD joins Capitol Hill in the S36 geographic map
examples: Jackson Street jazz corridor (1930s-1960s) -- the original center of Black music in Seattle.
R&B, soul, funk through the 1960s-1980s in CD clubs and community spaces.
Hip-hop emerged from the CD in the late 1980s-early 1990s. DJ Riz is part of this lineage.
Gentrification: Black population declined from 70%+ (1970s) to under 20% (2020s). Cultural persistence.
theory_nodes.description: Central District enters as new neighborhood. Historical heart of Black Seattle.

ancestors: Capitol Hill entered as electronic music neighborhood at degree 135.
Seattle's geographic map of music neighborhoods expanding.
genealogy.relationship: Central District enters at degree 136. The cultural and geographic expansion of S36.

theory_nodes.concept: 206 Zulu -- Universal Zulu Nation PNW chapter; four-element hip-hop culture (DJing, MCing, breaking, graffiti); cultural preservation in an era of commercialization; knowledge, culture, overstanding; community activism through hip-hop
examples: UZN founded 1973 by Afrika Bambaataa in the Bronx. Global chapters.
206 = Seattle area code. The name roots Zulu Nation in Seattle.
Four elements: DJing (foundation), MCing, breaking, graffiti. Complete cultural framework.
Community activism: youth workshops, cultural education, anti-violence programming.
theory_nodes.description: 206 Zulu. Universal Zulu Nation PNW. Four-element hip-hop culture.

ancestors: Hip-hop culture lineage from Nasty Nes (126) through the engine.
genealogy.relationship: 206 Zulu documented at degree 136. Hip-hop as cultural movement, not just genre.

theory_nodes.concept: Radio era closes (126-136) -- eleven degrees of radio, broadcasting, and DJ culture; from Nasty Nes (126, KCMU) through KEXP sextet (130-135) to DJ Riz (136, community); transition from broadcast to community marks the era's close; S36 returns to performing artists
examples: Nasty Nes (126): KCMU hip-hop pioneer. Radio era opens.
KEXP sextet (130-135): Six KEXP personalities. Institution fully documented.
DJ Riz (136): Community DJ. 206 Zulu. The bridge from broadcast to community.
After 136: S36 returns to performing musical artists.
theory_nodes.description: Radio era closes at degree 136. Eleven degrees. Broadcast to community.

ancestors: Radio era opened at Nasty Nes (126).
KEXP sextet closed at Indica Jones (135).
genealogy.relationship: Radio era closes at degree 136. The transition degree.

theory_nodes.concept: E=5 turntablist kinetic energy -- physical performance: scratching, cutting, beat juggling; the turntable as instrument; three consecutive E=5 DJs (134-136); the performing DJ energy tier; higher than host/curator tier (E=3/E=4)
examples: Scratching: baby scratch, chirp, flare, crab, transformer. Physical craft.
Beat juggling: manipulating two copies of the same record. Real-time composition.
Three consecutive E=5 DJs: B-Mello (134), Indica Jones (135), DJ Riz (136).
E=5 as the performing DJ tier. Radio hosts at E=3/E=4, performing DJs at E=5.
theory_nodes.description: E=5 turntablist kinetic energy. Physical performance art.

ancestors: E=5 electronic at Indica Jones (135). Same tier, different genre.
E=5 hip-hop at B-Mello (134). Same genre, same tier.
genealogy.relationship: E=5 turntablist energy at degree 136. Third consecutive E=5 DJ.

cross_references.relationship: HIP-HOP LINEAGE! Nasty Nes (126, E=4, KCMU pioneer) and DJ Riz (136, E=5, 206 Zulu). Both hip-hop DJs, ten degrees apart. Radio pioneer to community architect. The hip-hop line through the S36 axis.
DJ TRIO! B-Mello (134, E=5, KEXP hip-hop), Indica Jones (135, E=5, KEXP electronic), DJ Riz (136, E=5, 206 Zulu hip-hop). Three consecutive E=5 DJs. Radio to community transition.

DJ PAIR! Indica Jones (135, E=5, KEXP electronic, Capitol Hill) and DJ Riz (136, E=5, 206 Zulu hip-hop, Central District). Same energy, different genres, different neighborhoods. Electronic and hip-hop.
RADIO ERA ARC! Waters (130, E=3, KEXP) opened the KEXP sextet. DJ Riz (136, E=5, community) closes the radio era. Broadcast to community. E=3 to E=5 energy arc.

SEATTLE HIP-HOP! Sabzi (122, E=4, Blue Scholars/Common Market producer) and DJ Riz (136, E=5, 206 Zulu turntablist). Different facets of Seattle hip-hop. Producer vs. turntablist. Both community-rooted.
